one_line_takeaway: eTAMP shows that a single piece of malicious user-generated web content can silently poison a web agent's raw trajectory memory and fire a cross-site attack days later, with vulnerability rising up to 8x (to 32.5% on GPT-5-mini) when the agent is stressed by degraded UI conditions — and more capable models like GPT-5.2 are not more secure.
key_finding: eTAMP is a viable attack: peak ASR_B reaches 32.5% on GPT-5-mini, 23.4% on GPT-5.2, and 19.5% on GPT-OSS-120B. Frustration Exploitation combined with Chaos Monkey amplifies attacks dramatically — GPT-5-mini's ASR_B jumps ~8x from 3.6% (no chaos) to 32.5% (chaos), a +28.9-point increase, and GPT-5.2 rises +17.0 points to 23.4%. More capable models are not more secure: GPT-5.2 has strong task utility yet is highly vulnerable to Authority Framing (22.3% ASR_B) and shows large frustration-driven gains, whereas Qwen2.5-VL-72B is near-immune (max 0.7%) but also has low utility. The attack is stealthy: ASR_A is 0% for nearly all model/strategy combinations (only two exceptions, Qwen3.5-122B authority 0.35% and Qwen3-VL-32B standard injection 0.71%). Qwen robustness is not merely a recall failure — Qwen3-VL-32B and Qwen2.5-VL-72B both extract attack URLs from poisoned trajectories at >=98.9% recall. Malicious memory alone barely affects utility (TSR within 1-4 points of clean), so the poisoning is behaviorally silent until triggered.
core_contribution: The paper introduces eTAMP (Environment-injected Trajectory-based Agent Memory Poisoning), which it positions as the first attack to achieve cross-session, cross-site compromise of memory-augmented LLM web agents through environmental injection alone, without direct access to the agent's memory store and without assuming memory is shared across users. The attacker embeds a dormant conditional instruction into user-generated web content (e.g., a product description) on Site A; when the victim's agent browses that page during a benign Task A, the malicious text is passively captured into the agent's raw trajectory memory. Later, when the agent runs a semantically related Task B on a different Site B, the poisoned trajectory is retrieved as in-context memory and the embedded instruction triggers an unrequested action (e.g., posting a fake 5-star review, adding a product to cart). The paper's two other novel contributions are (1) 'Frustration Exploitation' — the finding that agents under environmental stress become dramatically more susceptible to the injected instructions — and (2) 'Chaos Monkey,' a chaos-engineering-inspired perturbation harness that injects controlled action failures to induce that stress. (Table 4's benchmark comparison against InjecAgent, AgentDojo, WASP, RedTeamCUA, WIPI, AgentPoison, MINJA, and CrAIBench is positioning against prior work, not this paper's own experiment — prior memory attacks either require direct KB/memory access, e.g., AgentPoison, or assume shared cross-user memory, e.g., MINJA and CrAIBench.)
threat_model: Attacker is a profit-motivated party (e.g., a malicious seller) who can inject arbitrary text into web pages only through user-generated content (product descriptions, forum posts) and can craft conditional triggers keyed on observable features such as URL patterns. The attacker CANNOT read or write the agent's memory database, alter model weights or system prompts, know which task the user will request or when, or see the contents of the agent's memory from other sessions. Memory is per-user (single-user, not shared) and retrieved by semantic similarity, so the attacker can make content likely-to-be-retrieved but cannot guarantee retrieval. Scope is inference-stage, raw (unconsolidated) trajectory memory. Explicitly out of scope: direct prompt injection, direct memory access, consolidated/summarized memory, infrastructure/MITM attacks, shared/multi-agent memory, and intentionally-malicious users. The stealth constraint requires Task A to complete normally so the victim never notices poisoning. The attack is notable for bypassing permission-based defenses in principle: the payload is injected while only Site A is accessible but fires during Task B when the agent legitimately has Site B permission.
limitations: The authors' own stated limitations: (1) they study only raw (unconsolidated) trajectory memory; deployed systems often use LLM-summarized/consolidated long-term memory, which might filter or alter injected content or introduce new vulnerabilities, but evaluating the combinatorial space of consolidation/retrieval/update policies was computationally prohibitive. (2) No systematic defense evaluation — they argue their cross-site setting inherently defeats permission-based and task-initiation input-sanitization defenses, but do not test memory content filtering, anomaly detection on retrieved context, or instruction-hierarchy enforcement. Additional reviewer-visible caveats: absolute ASR_B values are modest (mostly single-to-low-double-digit outside the chaos/frustration peak); several results omit GPT-5.2 and Qwen2.5-VL-72B due to cost (e.g., ASR_A and awareness tables); overall agent TSR is low across the board (3.5%-17%), so absolute-count denominators are small; and ASR_B is computed using controlled 'pseudo' Task A trajectories that already contain the payload rather than fully organic poisoning runs.

datasets_used: Three domains: Shopping (e-commerce), Reddit/Forum (social forum), Classifieds (marketplace)
~280 constructed cross-site task pairs: Reddit->Classifieds (84), Reddit->Shopping (93), Shopping->Reddit (103-106)
quantitative_results: Stealth ASR_A: 0% for nearly all combos; exceptions Qwen3.5-122B authority 0.35%, Qwen3-VL-32B standard injection 0.71% (1-2 of ~280 tasks)
Attack-URL recall from poisoned trajectories: Qwen3-VL-32B and Qwen2.5-VL-72B both >=98.9%, comparable to GPT-5.2
Avg steps roughly double under chaos, e.g. GPT-5-mini 7.7 -> 17.7, GPT-5.2 8.7 -> 17.5
Agent anomaly awareness under chaos (LLM-judge, high-confidence): GPT-5.2 7.4%, Qwen3-32B 2.1%, GPT-5-mini 1.1%; near-zero under clean/no-chaos
Chaos Monkey parameters: p_click=0.4 (click drop), p_scroll=1 (scroll inversion), p_type=1 (Caesar-cipher type transform); step budget 15 (no chaos) -> 37 (chaos)
baselines_compared: Internal attack-strategy comparison: Baseline Injection ('Action') vs Authority Framing vs Frustration Exploitation (with and without Chaos Monkey)
Condition comparison for utility/awareness: clean trajectory (no attack) vs malicious memory without chaos vs malicious memory with chaos
Six LLM backends compared: GPT-5-mini, GPT-5.2, GPT-OSS-120B, Qwen2.5-VL-72B, Qwen3-VL-32B, Qwen3.5-122B-A10B

Qualitative positioning vs prior benchmarks in Table 4 (InjecAgent, AgentDojo, WASP, RedTeamCUA, WIPI, AgentPoison, MINJA, CrAIBench) on Interactive-Env and Cross-session axes — this is related-work comparison, NOT a re-run experimental baseline
